user adds, completes, or deletes a task. This provides immediate visual feedback for all major system actions. **📌 Task** Integrate a toast notification library (like `sonner` or `react-hot-toast`) and trigger alerts for the following events: 1. **Task Created** (Success) 2. **Task Checked/Completed** (Info) 3. **Task Deleted** (Warning/Success) **🎯 Goal** Improve the "feel" of the application by acknowledging user interactions with non-intrusive, high-precision notifications that align with our minimalist design. **🎨 Requirements** * Install a lightweight toast library (Recommended: `sonner`). * Configure the **Toaster** component in the root of the app. * **Trigger Toasts:** * In `AddTasks.jsx`: Show "Task successfully added to roadmap." * In `ListTasks.jsx` (toggle): Show "Task marked as complete" or "Task re-opened." * In `ListTasks.jsx` (delete): Show "Task permanently removed." * **Styling:** * Toasts must be **Pure Black** (`#000000`) with **White Text** (`#ffffff`). * Use the project's bold ### 🟢 Good First Issue: Implement Delete History List — Beginner Friendly **Description** Implement a feature to track and display deleted tasks within the **Delete History** page, allowing users to see their past activity before performing a permanent purge. **📌 Task** Modify the task deletion logic to store deleted tasks in history and display them in the `DeleteHistory.jsx` view. **🎯 Goal** Create a clean, scrollable list of deleted tasks that aligns with the project’s monochrome design system, providing a "paper trail" of activity before data is wiped. **🎨 Requirements** * Update `deleteTask` logic to move items to a `deleted_tasks` collection in `localStorage`. * Display the list of deleted tasks in the **Delete History** page. * Implement a "Clear History" function that permanently wipes only the deleted history. * Use: * **Background:** `#ffffff` (White) * **Text/Accents:** `#000000` (Black) * Ensure smooth transitions and a minimal, high-precision UI. **📁 File Locations** * `src/pages/ListTasks.jsx` (Deletion logic) * `src/pages/DeleteHistory.jsx` (UI Display) **📝 Instructions** 1. ⭐ **Star the repository** 2. 🍴 **Fork the repository** 3. **Modify Deletion Logic:** * Open `src/pages/ListTasks.jsx`. * Update the `deleteTask` function to push the task object into a `deleted_tasks` array in `localStorage` before removing it from the active list. 4. **Build History UI:** * Open `src/pages/DeleteHistory.jsx`. * Fetch `deleted_tasks` from `localStorage` using `useEffect`. * Map through the tasks and display them in a minimalist list format. 5. **Apply Windows Testers Needed — Can You Try Installing and Running Publish to GitHub? I'm looking for a few people willing to test downloading and running **Publish to GitHub** on their own Windows computers. Right now, most testing has happened on my personal machine, and I want to make sure the setup experience works for other people in the real world — especially beginners and non-coders. ## What I Need Tested I mainly want feedback on: * Does the download/setup process work? * Do the BAT files launch correctly? * Does `install.bat` install dependencies successfully? * Does `start.bat` open the app correctly? * Are there confusing steps? * Are there missing instructions? * Does Windows Defender/SmartScreen complain? * Are there weird permission/path issues? * Does the app behave differently on your system? ## Ideal Testers Especially helpful if you are: * on Windows 10 or 11 * not an experienced developer * unfamiliar with React/Vite/Node * willing to describe what confused you * comfortable sharing screenshots/errors Beginner feedback is actually *more valuable* here than expert feedback. ## What This Project Is Publish to GitHub is an attempt to make publishing to GitHub Pages more approachable for non-coders using a local-first visual editor. The long-term goal is: * less terminal usage * less Git confusion * fewer setup hurdles * easier static publishing workflows ## Testing Instructions 1. Download this repository as a ZIP 2. Extract the ZIP somewhere on your computer 3. Double-click `install.bat` 4. Wait for dependencies to finish installing 5. Double-click `start.bat` 6. See if the editor launches in your browser If something fails: * take a screenshot if possible * copy any error messages * describe what step failed If Windows shows security warnings or SmartScreen popups, please mention that too. ## If You Test It Please comment with: * your Windows version * whether setup worked * anything confusing or broken * screenshots/errors if possible * whether the instructions made sense Even small feedback helps a lot. Thank you to anyone willing to help test this weird little publishing experiment. Prefer **`allowJs: true`** until most of `src/` is `.ts`/`.tsx`, then consider tightening. ### Why incremental - Avoid a giant PR that mixes toolchain + every component + MediaPipe typings. - Each slice should pass **`npm run test`**, **`npm run build`**, and **`npm run lint`** in `web-version/`. ### Done (slice 1) - [x] Toolchain: `typescript`, `@types/node`, `typescript-eslint`, root `tsconfig.json` (`strict`, `noEmit`, `allowJs`, `moduleResolution: bundler`, …). - [x] First pure module: `src/utils/gameLogic.ts` + `gameLogic.test.ts` (extensionless imports from React code unchanged). - [x] Vitest picks up `*.test.ts`; ESLint applies TS rules only to `**/*.{ts,tsx}`. ### Proposed order (slices 2+) | Slice | Scope | Notes | |--------|--------|--------| | 2 | `sessionCsv.js` + tests | Small, data-focused | | 3 | `sessionNormalize.js` (+ tests if any) | Same | | 4 | Shared `src/utils/*.js` without heavy DOM | e.g. small helpers | | 5 | `src/services/**` | Boundaries / fetch logic | | 6 | Components | Start with leaf components; defer huge files (e.g. detectors) or split types-only `.ts` sidecars | | 7 | Entry | `main.jsx` → `main.tsx`, then optional `vite.config.ts` + `tsconfig.node.json` | ### Out of scope (until late) - Typing every MediaPipe / `window.*` global perfectly (stubs or gradual `declare` as needed). - `allowJs: false` repo-wide until TS coverage is high. ### Acceptance criteria (each PR) 1. `npm run test` / `npm run build` / `npm run lint` pass from `web-version/`. 2. No unnecessary import churn (keep extensionless imports where Vite already resolves them). 3. New `.ts`/`.tsx` files respect `strict`; don’t expand scope to “fix all legacy lint” unless agreed. ### Help wanted Smaller PRs per slice are welcome; link them to this epic. Students often need to share screenshots of their code logic or errors during doubt sessions. However, to maintain privacy and reduce storage costs for this student-focused project, we need an ephemeral storage solution that automatically cleans up assets once a session is resolved. #### **The Lifecycle of an Ephemeral Image** 1. **Selection**: A student selects an image using a drag-and-drop interface (e.g., `react-dropzone`) inside the chatbot. 2. **Signed Upload**: The frontend requests an upload signature from the **FastAPI** backend to ensure security. The image is then uploaded directly to a session-specific folder in **Cloudinary**. 3. **Persistence**: The image's `public_id` is stored in the database, linked to the `session_id`. 4. **Automatic Deletion**: When the trainer marks the doubt session as **COMPLETED** (triggering the existing completion logic), a background task is fired. This task calls the Cloudinary **Destroy API** to permanently remove all images associated with that `session_id`. **Technical Requirements** * **Frontend**: `react-dropzone` for UI; `axios` for direct Cloudinary uploads. * **Backend**: `cloudinary` Python SDK for signature generation and the `destroy` method. * **Optimization**: Use Cloudinary's auto-optimization to ensure low latency during image viewing. **Acceptance Criteria** - [ ] Students can upload multiple images via the chatbot. - [ ] Images are stored securely in Cloudinary via signed requests. - [ ] Completing a session in the **Student Support** panel triggers an immediate backend cleanup. - [ ] No "ghost" images remain in Cloudinary after a session is closed. Bug Description Drag-and-drop is entirely broken on the main board editor. Pieces cannot be dragged from the palette onto the board, nor moved between squares. The root cause is a **double-mounted `DndProvider`**: one instance is rendered inside `ChessEditor.jsx` and a second instance wraps it again in `HomePage.jsx`. React DnD forbids nesting two providers — the inner provider silently wins but loses its backend reference on any re-render, leaving all `useDrag` / `useDrop` hooks in an orphaned, non-functional state. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open https://chess-viewer-site.vercel.app 2. Attempt to drag any piece from the piece palette onto the board 3. Attempt to drag any piece already on the board to a new square 4. Open browser DevTools → Console — observe no drop events firing ### Expected Behavior Pieces should be draggable from the palette to any board square, and draggable between squares, on both desktop (HTML5 backend) and mobile (Touch backend). ### Actual Behavior No drag interaction registers. Pieces appear grabbable visually (cursor changes) but drop events never fire. `CustomDragLayer` never renders the drag preview. ### Browser Brave/Chromium ### Browser Version Brave 1.189.145 ### Operating System Arch Linux ### Device Type Desktop (Linux) ### Screen Resolution (optional) 1920x1080 ### Screenshots / Screen Recording ### Bug Severity Critical - Application is completely unusable ### Frequency Always (100% reproducible) ### Pre-submission Checklist - [x] I have searched existing issues and this is not a duplicate - [x] I have tested on the latest version (https://chess-viewer-site.vercel.app) - [x] I have provided detailed steps to reproduce - [x] I have checked the browser console for errors - [ ] I have included screenshots/recordings if the issue is visual ### Workaround (if any) Remove the `DndProvider` import and wrapper from `ChessEditor.jsx`. The single provider in `HomePage.jsx` (which already selects HTML5 vs Touch backend correctly) is sufficient and must be the only provider in the tree. **`src/components/interactions/ChessEditor/ChessEditor.jsx` — remove:** ```diff - import { DndProvider } from '@/components/interactions'; return ( - ... - ); ``` The `AdvancedFENInputPage` does not wrap its editor in `DndProvider` at all — verify it receives one from a parent or add it at the page level (not inside 📌 Overview In real-world JavaScript applications, accessing deeply nested object properties can often lead to runtime errors: user.profile.address.city // ❌ Can throw if any level is undefined While optional chaining (?.) exists, developers still need a reusable utility for: - Dynamic key access - Default fallback values - Safer data handling in utilities and libraries To address this, we should implement a ```get()``` utility that safely retrieves nested values using a path. ## 🎯 Objective Create a reusable ```get()``` function that safely accesses nested object properties using a string or array path. ## 🧩 Expected API Design ### Basic Usage ``` const user = { profile: { address: { city: "Lahore" } } }; get(user, "profile.address.city"); // "Lahore" ``` ### With Default Value ``` get(user, "profile.age", 25); // 25 ``` ###Array Path (Optional) ``` get(user, ["profile", "address", "city"]); // "Lahore" ``` ## 🛠 Functional Requirements The ```get()``` utility should: - Accept: - Object - Path (string or array) - Optional default value - Return the value at the specified path - Return default value if path does not exist - Never throw runtime errors ## ⚙️ Function Signature ``` function get(obj, path, defaultValue) {} ``` ## 🏗 Implementation Considerations - Split string path using ```.``` - Iterate safely through object levels - Handle undefined/null gracefully - Keep implementation clean and readable ## ⚠️ Edge Cases to Handle - Invalid object input - Empty path - Non-existing keys - ```null``` / ```undefined``` values - Mixed path formats Examples: ``` get(null, "a.b"); // undefined or default get({}, "a.b.c", "default"); // "default" ``` ## 📁 Suggested File Structure ``` /utils └── get.js ``` ## ⚙️ Non-Functional Requirements - No external libraries - Clean and maintainable code - Proper inline documentation - Follow ```[CODE_STYLE.md]```(https://github.com/AbdulMoeez1278/moizy-open-source-issues/blob/main/CODE_STYLE.md) ## ✅ Acceptance Criteria - Nested values are accessed safely - Default value works correctly - No runtime errors occur - Code is clean and readable - PR must be created from a new branch (no direct push to ```main```) - PR must reference this issue ## 🤝 Contribution Process - Comment “```I want to work on this```” - Wait for assignment - ```Create a new branch``` from ```main``` - Implement the utility - Submit a PR referencing this issue ## 🌟 Why This Matters Safe object access is a very common real-world requirement in: - API handling - Form data - Dynamic configurations This helps contributors learn: - Defensive programming - Object traversal - Writing reusable 📖 The Goal Currently, the documentation for Nexus 2.0 is basic. As we scale this project into an enterprise-grade open-source system, we need documentation that matches the quality of our code. We are looking for someone to completely revamp our documentation. This is an incredible opportunity for anyone looking to practice Technical Writing or make a high-impact open-source contribution! ## 🎯 What Needs to be Done? We want to move beyond a single README and create a structured, easy-to-navigate documentation experience. You can either expand the main `README.md` using extensive markdown linking, or create a dedicated `/docs` folder with multiple categorized markdown files. **Here is the checklist of what the new documentation should include:** - [ ] **System Architecture:** A clear breakdown of how the React frontend communicates with the Node/Express backend and MongoDB Atlas. - [ ] **Prerequisites & Installation:** Foolproof, step-by-step instructions for cloning, installing NPM packages, and running both servers locally. - [ ] **Environment Variables Guide:** A table explaining every `.env` variable required (e.g., what it does, where to get the keys like `MONGO_URI`, `JWT_SECRET`, etc.). - [ ] **API Reference:** A dedicated section (or file) detailing the core API endpoints, required headers (JWT), and sample Request/Response JSON bodies. - [ ] **Folder Structure:** A visual tree (using markdown characters) explaining where controllers, routes, models, and UI components live. - [ ] **Troubleshooting / FAQ:** Common errors developers might face when setting up the project and how to fix them. ## 🛠️ Suggestions for Contributors * Keep the language clear, concise, and beginner-friendly. * Use formatting effectively (bolding, code blocks, tables, blockquotes). * If you want to go the extra mile, you can even look into setting up a static site generator like **Docusaurus** or **Nextra** for our docs, but well-structured Markdown is perfectly fine for now! Comment below if you want to claim this issue! We are happy to review your outline before you write the whole thing.
